data:
Search results for "PLY yacc shift reduce conflict resolution precedence example":

Title: Incorrect shift-reduce conflict resolution with precedence specifier
URL: https://github.com/dabeaz/ply/issues/101
Content: ## Use saved searches to filter your results more quickly. While learning to use PLY and experimenting with it I've ran into strange issue. However, the resolution path taken by PLY seems to be incorrect: PLY chooses to reduce rule instead of shifting next token, although token is of higher priority than the rule used for reduction. `if_stmt : IF stmt ELSE stmt FI. Token ELSE has higher precedence than IFX:. As such, I expect rule `if_stmt -> IF stmt` to have less priority than token ELSE, so shift-reduce conflict should be resolved by shifting (per documentation):. `If the current token has higher precedence than the rule on the stack, it is shifted.`. However, when shift-reduce conflict arises (state 11 in parser.out) PLY chooses to reduce using production `if_stmt -> IF stmt` instead of shifting ELSE token:. ELSE reduce using rule 7 (if_stmt -> IF stmt .). | IF stmt ELSE stmt FI`. ### Footer navigation.

Title: PLY (Python Lex-Yacc) - David Beazley
URL: https://www.dabeaz.com/ply/ply.html
Content: A shift/reduce conflict is caused when the parser generator can't decide whether or not to reduce a rule or shift a symbol on the parsing stack. For example,

Title: Resolving shift/reduce conflicts in ply yacc - Stack Overflow
URL: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/61751397/resolving-shift-reduce-conflicts-in-ply-yacc
Content: # compiler construction - Resolving shift/reduce conflicts in ply yacc - Stack Overflow. 1.   Stack Internal Implement a knowledge platform layer to power your enterprise and AI tools. 3. Releases Keep up-to-date on features we add to Stack Overflow and Stack Internal. *   About Us Learn more about Stack Overflow the company, and our products. I did that for one or two of the productions using an online tool but it added a lot of shift-reduce conflicts and a few reduce/reduce conflicts, I'm not really good at these things so I was skeptical whether I should continue eliminating all left recursions from all productions to see the result or the early bad result was a stop sign. ! shift/reduce conflict for OR resolved as shift. ! shift/reduce conflict for SUB resolved as shift. WARNING: shift/reduce conflict for WHILE in state 72 resolved as shift.

Content: When a state has both a shift and a reduce action for the. same token, we say there is a shift/reduce conﬂict. In this case, the conﬂict. will arise, for

Title: How to resolve parser conflicts - Learning - OCaml
URL: https://discuss.ocaml.org/t/how-to-resolve-parser-conflicts/9314
Content: # How to resolve parser conflicts. `Warning: 9 states have shift/reduce conflicts. Warning: 2 states have reduce/reduce conflicts. Warning: 4 reduce/reduce conflicts were arbitrarily resolved.`. | BFUNC expr { FuncApp ($1, $2) }. | expr POW expr { ExpoApp ($1, $3) }. | expr MULT expr { Mult ($1, $3) }. | expr DIV expr { Mult ($1, ExpoApp ($3, Int (-1))) }. | expr PLUS expr { Add ($1, $3) }. | expr MINUS expr { Add ($1, Mult (Int (-1), $3)) }. | MINUS expr %prec SIGN { Mult (Int (-1), $2) }. | expr expr %prec PRODUCT { Mult ($1, $2) }. Your grammar has reduce-reduce conflicts stemming from the last rule `expr -> expr expr`. For example, `expr PLUS expr` can be reduced and produce either a “`Mult`” or an “`Add`”. I think what @nojb is trying to say, is that there is a basic understanding of how to write LR(1) (in the case of Menhir and Yacc) grammars, that you probably should gain.
